critique.summary: The article describes a comprehensive, regularly updated dataset containing the complete archive of Hacker News posts and comments from 2006 to March 2026. The dataset is stored in Parquet format, updated every 5 minutes, and is accessible via Hugging Face with tools like DuckDB and the datasets library for easy querying and analysis.
mainPoints: The dataset includes every Hacker News item since October 2006, totaling over 47 million items and 11.6 GB of data.
Data is updated live every 5 minutes via the Hacker News Firebase API and monthly authoritative files are refreshed from a ClickHouse mirror.
The dataset is organized by month and includes metadata such as item type, author, score, and timestamps.
It supports various use cases including language model training, sentiment analysis, and research on online community dynamics.
The dataset preserves all content as it appears publicly on Hacker News, including deleted and flagged items, and is released under the Open Data Commons Attribution License.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are accurate and verifiable. Hacker News is a well-known platform operated by Y Combinator, and the dataset described is hosted on Hugging Face, a reputable platform for datasets. The use of the ClickHouse Playground as a source for Hacker News data is consistent with publicly available information. The technical details about data format, update frequency, and usage instructions align with standard practices for large-scale data archiving and analysis. No unsupported or false claims were identified.
considerations: The dataset includes usernames and user-generated content without additional anonymization, which may raise privacy concerns despite being publicly available data.
The Hacker News community has known demographic and topical biases that may affect analyses using this dataset.
Scores reflect snapshots at the time of data sync and may not represent final or current values.
The dataset does not include user profile information such as karma or biographies.
HTML formatting in text fields may require preprocessing for certain types of analysis.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the release of a large Hacker News archive dataset in Parquet format, updated frequently. Participants debate the technical choices, legal concerns, and potential uses of the dataset, with some highlighting limitations and others expressing enthusiasm for the resource.

comments.author: 6thbit
comments.text:
From YC /legal

> Except as expressly authorized by Y Combinator, you agree not to modify, copy, frame, scrape, rent, lease, loan, sell, distribute or create derivative works based on the Site or the Site Content, in whole or in part

Not to pretend this isn't widely happening behind the curtains already, but coming from a "Show HN" seems daring.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately highlights the legal restrictions imposed by Y Combinator on Hacker News content and the tension between those terms and common practices.

comments.author: 0cf8612b2e1e
comments.text:
Under the Known Limitations section

  deleted and dead are integers. They are stored as 0/1 rather than booleans.
Is there a technical reason to do this? You have the type right there.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid technical question about data representation which could impact efficiency or compatibility, showing attention to detail.

comments.author: gkbrk
comments.text: My Hacker News items table in ClickHouse has 47,428,860 items, and it's 5.82 GB compressed and 18.18 GB uncompressed. What makes Parquet compression worse here, when both formats are columnar?
comments.analysis: Provides a relevant comparison of storage formats and compression, prompting useful discussion about performance trade-offs.

comments.author: vovavili
comments.text: Replacing an 11.6GB Parquet file every 5 minutes strikes me as a bit wasteful. I would probably use Apache Iceberg here.
comments.analysis: Offers a constructive suggestion to improve efficiency by using a more suitable data management format for frequent updates.

comments.author: trwhite
comments.text: Hello. I didn’t consent to any of my HN comments being used in this way. Please kindly remove them.
comments.analysis: Expresses a legitimate privacy and consent concern regarding the use of user-generated content in datasets.

comments.author: politician
comments.text: This is great. I've soured on this site over the past few years due to the heavy partisanship that wasn't as present in the early days (eternal September), but there are still quite a few people whose opinions remain thought-provoking and insightful.  I'm going to use this corpus to make a local self-hosted version of HN with the ability to a) show inline article summaries and b) follow those folks.
comments.analysis: Shows a practical and positive use case for the dataset, demonstrating its value for personal projects.

comments.author: Imustaskforhelp
comments.text:
As someone who had made a project analysing hackernews who had used clickhouse, I really feel like this is a project made for me (especially the updated every 5 minute aspect which could've helped my project back then too!)

Your project actually helps me out a ton in making one of the new project ideas that I had about hackernews that I had put into the back-burner.

I had thought of making a ping website where people can just @Username and a service which can detect it and then send mail to said username if the username has signed up to the service (similar to a service run by someone from HN community which mails you everytime someone responds to your thread directly, but this time in a sort of ping)

[The previous idea came as I tried to ping someone to show them something relevant and thought that wait a minute, something like ping which mails might be interesting and then tried to see if I can use algolia or any service to hook things up but not many/any service made much sense back then sadly so I had the idea in back of my mind but this service sort of solves it by having it being updated every 5 minutes]

Your 5 minute updates really make it possible. I will look what I can do with that in some days but I am seeing some discrepancy in the 5 minute update as last seems to be 16 march in the readme so I would love to know more about if its being updated every 5 minutes because it truly feels phenomenal if true and its exciting to think of some new possibilities unlocked with it.
comments.analysis: Provides user perspective on the dataset’s usefulness and raises a valid question about update frequency accuracy.

comments.author: lhoestq
comments.text: Clickhouse should implement Parquet CDC to enable deduplication and faster uploads/downloads on HF
comments.analysis: Suggests a technical improvement that could optimize data handling, showing knowledge of database technologies.

comments.author: palmotea
comments.text:
> At midnight UTC, the entire current month is refetched from the source as a single authoritative Parquet file, and today's individual 5-minute blocks are removed from the today/ directory.

Wouldn't that lose deleted/moderated comments?
comments.analysis: Raises a valid concern about data integrity and completeness related to the update strategy.

comments.author: Onavo
comments.text: Is is possible to only download a subset? e.g. Show HNs or HN Whoishiring. The Show HNs and HN Whoishiring are very useful for classroom data science i.e. a very useful set of data for students to learn the basic of data cleaning and engineering.
comments.analysis: Requests a practical feature to improve usability for educational purposes, reflecting user needs.
